coredata.dc:title: High-performance porphyrin-like graphene quantum dots for immuno-sensing of Salmonella typhi
coredata.dc:creator: Kamal, Zahra
Zarei Ghobadi, Mohadeseh
Mohseni, Seyed Majid
Ghourchian, Hedayatollah
coredata.dc:description: The extraordinary optical properties of porphyrins have inspired their applications in various fields. Herein, we introduce iron porphyrin bio-mimicked graphene quantum dots (Fe–N-GQDs) as a novel paramagnetic and fluorescent label. The Fe–N-GQD was prepared by the mechanochemical mixing of Fe, N, and C sources followed by pyrolysis at high-temperature and next, the solvothermal treatment was performed. The Fe–N sites in graphene matrix, the structural alterations during the solvothermal treatment, the optical properties, and paramagnetic behaviour were studied using FTIR, Raman and X-ray spectroscopies, and Vibrating sample magnetometer. The structural studies revealed that under solvothermal condition, Fe–N doped graphene sheets cut into ultra-small Fe–N-GQDs containing well-dispersed particles with an average diameter of about 2.5 nm. As a result of Fe–N doping, the photoluminescence quantum yield was enhanced to 86% and strong paramagnetic behaviour was observed. Due to the rich oxygen-containing groups at Fe–N-GQDs surface, it has proper sites for bio-conjugation. The bioconjugated Fe–N-GQDs serve as donors in a prominent fluorescence resonance energy transfer system, while graphene oxide acts as an acceptor. The proposed immunosensor was successfully applied for the detection of Salmonella Typhi Vi antigen in real human serum in the concentration range from 1 pg/mL to 1 μg/mL with the detection limit of 1 pg/mL.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mechanochemical mixing
Iron-nitrogen doping
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
Paramagnetic
Vi antigen

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Immobilization of antibody on Fe–N-GQDs surface
FRET mechanism for Vi antigen detection
Detection of Vi antigen in real human serum

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the Research Council of the University of Tehran .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of a) Fe–N-G, b,c) Fe–N-GQDs with different magnifications (50 and 20 nm). d) TEM-derived size distributions of Fe–N-GQDs (average size, 2.5 nm). e) AFM image of Fe–N-GQDs. f) Height profile along the green line in e. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Comparative study of XPS spectra of Fe–N-G and Fe–N-GQDs to monitor the structural changes by the solvothermal treatment. a) XPS survey spectra for Fe–N-G and Fe–N-GQDs. The XPS spectra from b to g indicate the C 1s, N 1s, Fe 2p, and O 1s for Fe–N-Gr and Fe–N-GQDs. In f, the vertical dashed lines indicate the Fe 2p3/2 band at 710.5 cm−1 and Fe 2p1/2 band at 723.5 cm−1 for both samples.

a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of aqueous Fe–N-GQDs. Inset: photographs of Fe–N-GQDs aqueous solution taken under the daylight (left) and LED light (center and right) at 360-nm excitation. b) The dependency of Fe–N-GQDs PL intensity on excitation wavelengths changes (from 340 to 400 nm). c) Effect of solvents on the PL intensity of Fe–N-GQDs at 360 nm excitation.

a) Overlapping the spectra of Fe–N-GQDs emission and GO absorption. b) The PL intensity changes due to the alteration of the distances between Fe–N-GQDs and GO. c) The fluorescence quenching spectra of Fe–N-GQDs due to the GO concentration increasing from top to bottom: 0, 2.5, 10, 15, and 20 μg/mL. Inset: The quenching efficiency induced by various concentrations of GO. d) Calibration curve of the FRET-based immunosensor for detection of S. typhi Vi antigen. e) Comparison between the specificity of FRET assay towards Vi Antigen and other non-specific targets: S. typhimurium (S.tm), E. coli (E.co), P. aeruginosa (P.ae) and S. aureus (S.au). f) Applicability of the immunosensor was examined using Vi antigen spiked with human serum at six different concentrations from 1 pg/mL to 1 μg/mL and the results were compared with the spiked with PBS.

The steps for synthesis of Fe–N-G (a) and Fe–N-GQD (b). a) The mixture of Fe2SO4, 1,10-phenanthroline and ZIF-8 was dried and ball-milled. The resulting powder was pyrolyzed at 1050 °C under argon gas for 1 h, yielding Fe–N-G. b) Fe–N-G was dissolved in DMF and then transferred to a poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4.5 h. The mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and a brown solution was obtained. The solvent was removed with the aid of a rotary evaporator to prepare Fe–N-GQDs.

The FRET mechanism for GO as an acceptor and Fe–N-GQDs as a donor. In the first step, GO comes into the proximity of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab and quenches its PL. In the upward process, in the presence of target (Vi Ag), due to the specific antibody–antigen interaction, the distance between Fe–N-GQDs and GO effectively increases, and PL is recovered. However, in the absence of target, PL remains quenched (the downward process).

head.ce:title: High-performance porphyrin-like graphene quantum dots for immuno-sensing of Salmonella typhi

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Biochemistry and Biophysics, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Biochemistry and Biophysics, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran, 1983969411, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Shahid Beheshti University, Tehran 1983969411, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Biochemistry and Biophysics, University of Tehran, P.O.Box 13145-1384, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The extraordinary optical properties of porphyrins have inspired their applications in various fields. Herein, we introduce iron porphyrin bio-mimicked graphene quantum dots (Fe–N-GQDs) as a novel paramagnetic and fluorescent label. The Fe–N-GQD was prepared by the mechanochemical mixing of Fe, N, and C sources followed by pyrolysis at high-temperature and next, the solvothermal treatment was performed. The Fe–N sites in graphene matrix, the structural alterations during the solvothermal treatment, the optical properties, and paramagnetic behaviour were studied using FTIR, Raman and X-ray spectroscopies, and Vibrating sample magnetometer. The structural studies revealed that under solvothermal condition, Fe–N doped graphene sheets cut into ultra-small Fe–N-GQDs containing well-dispersed particles with an average diameter of about 2.5 nm. As a result of Fe–N doping, the photoluminescence quantum yield was enhanced to 86% and strong paramagnetic behaviour was observed. Due to the rich oxygen-containing groups at Fe–N-GQDs surface, it has proper sites for bio-conjugation. The bioconjugated Fe–N-GQDs serve as donors in a prominent fluorescence resonance energy transfer system, while graphene oxide acts as an acceptor. The proposed immunosensor was successfully applied for the detection of Salmonella Typhi Vi antigen in real human serum in the concentration range from 1 pg/mL to 1 μg/mL with the detection limit of 1 pg/mL.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional single/multi-layered nanomaterials that have been noticed as fluorescent labels (Wang et al., 2016). On the other hand, graphene as a two-dimensional nanomaterial does not demonstrate photoluminescence properties due to a zero bandgap with an unlimited exciton Bohr radius (Benítez-Martínez and ). During the transformation of graphene to zero-dimensional quantum dots, new features emerge as a result of eminent quantum confinement and edge effects. Consequently, graphene quantum dots have a superior surface-to-volume ratio, higher solubility and chemical stability, more tendency to be functionalized, sharper and tuneable photoluminescence (PL), and premier hybridization with other nanomaterials (Lin et al., 2014). GQDs have overtaken the customary fluorophores and semiconductor quantum dots in various fields like optical bio-sensing, bio-imaging, and photovoltaics. Moreover, their PL is tuneable because of their size-dependent bandgaps. Generally, the PL properties of GQDs govern the light emitted by inherent and/or defect states. The zigzag or armchair edge, quantum confinement, recombination of localized electron–hole pairs in one hand, and energy trap states due to chemical functional groups, heteroatom dopants, and/or vacancy defects, on the other hand, are involved in this process (Zhu et al., 2017). In addition, doping of graphene materials by metals like Fe, Al, or Mn leads to omitting the zero bandgap energy and improving the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) (Cortés-Arriagada, 2016). As an example, porphyrins, the pigments of life (Battersby, 2000), are a group of heterocyclic macrocycle nitrogen-containing compounds with broad delocalized π electrons which intensely adsorb visible light. The coordination of metals to the nitrogen atoms in the center of porphyrins can significantly improve their optical features (Gao et al., 2018). So far, many nanocomposites containing porphyrins and carbon-based nanomaterials with various applications have been introduced (Rabiee et al., 2020). In previous studies, the binding of iron nanoparticles on the surface of nitrogen-GQDs and also graphene quantum dot-porphyrin composites were reported (Gao et al., 2018). However, iron-nitrogen doped GQDs (Fe–N GQDs) with an iron porphyrin-like structure has not been reported, yet. It is envisaged that this bio-mimicked GQD has photo-absorption privilege like porphyrin and shows high photoluminescence QY. GQDs with such high QY have good opportunities to use in practical applications such as bio-sensing, bio-imaging, drug delivery. photocatalyst, solar cells, optoelectronics, etc (Chen et al., 2016; Imran et al., 2018). Fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a potent technique to trace the interactions between various biomolecules through monitoring the energy transfer between donor-acceptor pairs. GQDs with promising properties like the high photo/chemical stability are candidates as good donors with efficient FRET for the detection of various biomolecules. Contrarily, the water-soluble graphene oxide (GO) is a superior fluorescence quencher and acceptor probe in FRET assay (Tian et al., 2017).

Salmonella enterica is a gram-negative Bacillus bacteria that causes food-borne disease. The consumption of contaminated food and products such as meat, poultry, and fresh products are the main source of Salmonellosis infections. Salmonella Typhi (S. Typhi) is the major member of a group of species that causes enteric fever or typhoid. The worldwide burden of typhoid fever has been evaluated to be almost 12.5–16.3 million cases resulting in 1–4% deaths in 2017 (Awol et al., 2021). The Widal test is routinely employed for the detection of S. Typhi in most laboratories but it suffers from low sensitivity and specificity. Therefore, there is a need to develop a fast, reliable, selective, sensitive, and user-friendly diagnostic test for S. Typhi (Pathania et al., 2018).

In the present work, the method for synthesis of Fe–N-GQDs is introduced and the characteristics of high photoluminescence QY, strong paramagnetic behaviour, and excellent chemical stability are confirmed. To prove the applicability of Fe–N-GQDs as a prominent FRET donor, a new immunosensor is proposed for the detection of S. Typhi-specific Vi antigen.

Zinc nitrate tetrahydrate, 2-methylimidazole (2-MeI), dimethylformamide (DMF), chloroform, 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen), ferrous sulfate (FeSO4 . 7H2O), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), chloroacetic acid (ClCH2COOH), N-ethyl-N′-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), and graphene oxide (GO) were purchased from Merck, Germany. Salmonella enterica serovar Typhi Vi capsular antigen (Vi antigen, Vi Ag) was purchased from Institute of Merieux, France. Anti-Vi antibody was procured from Denka Seiken Co., Ltd Coventry (UK). AmiconUltra10 centrifugal tubes and 0.22 μm syringe filters were purchased from Merck Millipore, USA. Pure water with a resistance of higher than 18 MΩ/cm was used in all experiments.

Fe–N-GQDs were prepared through a solvothermal approach (Liu et al., 2013). Firstly, Fe–N-G was dissolved in DMF (Some details for parental material synthesis can be found in the supplementary data, Scheme 1a ). The mixture solution was ultrasonicated for 30 min (120 W, 100 kHz) and then transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 4.5 h. It was cooled down to room temperature and then, filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to exclude the black precipitates. The resultant brown solvent was evaporated by a rotary evaporator (Scheme 1b). The obtained Fe–N-GQDs were dispersed in deionized water and used for further characterizations.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed with 208S Philips/FEI TEM (Philips, The Netherlands). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) was carried out using an ENTEGRA AFM (NT-MDT Co. Moscow, Russia). Raman spectra were measured on a Takram P50C0R10 spectrometer with a wavelength inciFdent laser light of 532 nm (Teksan Co. Tehran, Iran). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) of the Fe–N-GQDs was carried out on a Thermo Nicolet FTIR Spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) from 650 to 4000 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data was recorded on a Philips PW1730 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation. The magnetic properties were characterized by a Vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) at room temperature (Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir Co., Kashan, Iran). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies Inc., USA). The photoluminescence intensity was measured by a fluorescence Synergy H4 Hybrid Multi-Mode microplate reader (BioTek, USA) at room temperature. The PL quantum yield (QY) was determined by comparing the integrated PL intensities (excited at 360 nm) and the absorbance values using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as the reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of antibody on Fe–N-GQDs surface
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the conjugation of antibody to Fe–N-GQDs (abbreviated as Fe–N-GQDs/Ab), the carboxylic acid groups (-COOH) on Fe–N-GQDs were increased through the conversion of hydroxyl groups into carboxylic acid moieties (Zhao et al., 2013). Briefly, 2.5 mg NaOH and 5 mg ClCH2COOH were added into 1 mL Fe–N-GQDs solution, followed by bath sonication for 3 h to convert hydroxyl groups into carboxyl groups. The resultant product was neutralized with HCl. Then, 600 μL of Fe–N-GQD solution was mixed with 200 μL EDC (10 mg/mL) and 150 μL NHS (15 mg/mL). The mixture was kept at room temperature for 20 min. Then, 2 μL anti-Vi antibody S. Typhi (1 μg/mL) in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) was added into the mixture. The samples were shaken slowly at 25 °C for 2 h in the dark and kept overnight at 4 °C to allow Ab to covalently bind the Fe–N-GQDs surface. After that, the conjugate was washed five times with PBS (0.1M sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.4 containing 0.9% NaCl). Then, AmiconUltra10 centrifugal tubes were utilized to remove free unconjugated molecules by ultracentrifugation.

The capsular polysaccharide anti-phagocytic Vi antigen is the significant virulence factor of S. Typhi. Therefore, it can be considered as the antigen target for the detection of S. Typhi (13). To detect the Vi antigen, the quenching effect and fluorescence recovery of Fe–N-GQDs were monitored in the following steps. Firstly, 50 μL of Fe–N-GQDs-Ab suspension was taken in Eppendorf tube and mixed with 10 μL of GO solution (1 mg/mL). After 20 min incubation at room temperature, PL intensity was measured at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Next, a serial dilution of Vi antigen (1 pg/mL to 1 μg/mL) in human serum was added to Fe–N-GQDs-Ab/GO complex and incubated for 1 h. Then, the PL intensity of the complex was measured at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The specificity of the biosensor was also surveyed with various non-specific samples. The feasibility of the biosensor was examined using spiked human serum samples in comparison with spiked PBS samples. All the experiments were carried out thrice (n = 3).

Electron microscopy was used to study the morphology of Fe–N-G and Fe–N-GQDs. TEM image in Fig. 1a shows that Fe–N-G as the parent material of Fe–N-GQDs has an interrelated hollower and foam-like porous structure with a lateral size of about 300 nm (red arrows), which is similar to that reported in previous studies (Proietti et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2012). The Fe–N-G sheets under solvothermal condition cut into ultra-small GQDs. The TEM images (Fig. 1b and c) illustrate that produced Fe–N-GQDs are well-dispersed particles with an average diameter of about 2.5 nm (Fig. 1d). Moreover, Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) reveals that the average thickness of Fe–N-GQDs is ~ 3.3 nm corresponding to a few layers of graphene sheets (Fig. 1e and f).

In addition, Raman spectra of Fe–N-graphene and also after solvothermal cutting and converting to Fe–N-GQDs are illustrated in Fig. S1a. These spectra show two typical peaks at 1355 cm−1 and 1592 cm−1 corresponding to the D-disorder band (sp3) and G-band (sp2). The G band is due to in-plane vibrations of sp2 bonds of carbon atoms which indicates the degree of graphitic phase, while the D band is the result of out-of-plane vibrations attributed to the presence of structural defects (Gao et al., 2018). Therefore, the intensity of the D band increases, while the sp2 structure is destroyed and more sp3 bonds are formed. The relative intensity of D band to G band (ID/IG) for Fe–N-GQDs and Fe–N-G was calculated as 1.25 and 0.94, respectively. It is thus concluded that the solvothermal cutting of graphene merely leads to more structural defects due to the increase of the edge effects in GQDs (Pan et al., 2012). In the case of Fe–N-G, the peaks are found to be broadened in comparison with Fe–N-GQDs. The structural defects in graphene are more varied, but during the cutting of graphene sheets, this diversity is reduced and the more stable structure remains. This is why a sharper peak is observed in Fe–N-GQDs.

Further investigation on the effect of solvothermal treatment on Fe–N-G and Fe–N-GQDs functional groups was carried out by FTIR. The spectrum in Fig. S1b reveals that the solvothermal treatment affects –OH, CO, and C–N functional groups and causes structural defects in Fe–N-G while changing into Fe–N-GQDs. The FTIR spectrum of Fe–N-G revealed peaks at around 3272 and 1736 cm−1 corresponding to O–H and CO stretching frequencies of the –COOH group, respectively. The peaks corresponding to aromatic CC bending, phenolic C–O stretching, and epoxy C–O–C stretching are identified at 1534, 1243, and 1028 cm−1, respectively (Verma and Dutta, 2015). The absorption peaks at 867 and 827 cm−1 are consistent with the vibrations of –CH2 in the graphene plane of Fe–N-G and Fe–N-GQDs, respectively. The peak at 1400 cm−1 related to C–N groups corroborates the prosperous preparation of the N-doped graphitic materials (Gao et al., 2018).

The as-prepared Fe–N-GQD is found to be O-rich material with FTIR peaks at ca. 3383 cm−1 (νO-H), 1650 cm−1 (νC = O), 1335 cm−1 (νC-OH), and 1160 cm−1 (νC-O) related to the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups (Xu et al., 2015). After solvothermal treatment, the intensity of the epoxy group (1028 cm−1) in Fe–N-G decreases while the intensity of peaks related to carbonyl groups in Fe–N-GQDs increase. These alterations seem to be related to the reduction of epoxy group on the surface of GQDs. The significant increase of hydrophilic carboxyl groups including O–H and CO on the surface of Fe–N-GQDs causes good water solubility, unlike Fe–N-G (Wang et al., 2017).

As illustrated in Fig. S1c, the XRD pattern indicates a typical broad peak centered at 23.4° which is the characteristic of highly disordered carbon material (Vandarkuzhali et al., 2018). According to Bragg's law (Mahyari et al., 2016), the lattice spacing for Fe–N-GQD was computed to be 0.38 nm. It suggests an uncompact structure and π−π stacking (Faraji et al., 2018) perhaps owing to the high oxygen content of Fe–N-GQDs (Dong et al., 2012).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was also carried out to determine the surface contents and chemical configurations of the Fe–N-G and Fe–N-GQD nanomaterials. The XPS survey spectra in Fig. 2a shows a predominant graphitic C 1s peak at about 284.6 eV, an N 1s peak at about 401 eV, and an O 1s peak at about 532 eV confirming the prosperous doped graphene synthesis and changes in the ratio of content during GQDs synthesis (Liu et al., 2016). The comparison of C 1s in Fig. 2b demonstrates the obvious change in the carbon chemical environment from Fe–N-G to Fe–N-GQDs. The result of deconvolution treatment for the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2b) of Fe–N-G reveals that three peaks at 284.6 (CC, sp2 bonded carbon), 286.3 (C–OH, hydroxyls), and 285.7 (C–N) are well consistent with the high N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2c). It indicates the formation of graphene π-conjugated network with the presence of structural defects with oxygen and nitrogen groups. Furthermore, a new peak at 287.8 eV is presented in the C 1s spectrum of Fe–N-GQDs (Fig. 2c), which is attributed to the C bound to oxygen in the form of CO, carbonyls, revealing the formation of the carbonyl group at the edge of Fe–N-GQDs during solvothermal treatment of Fe–N-G. At the same time, the peak area of C–N (285.7 eV) is increased in Fe–N-GQD compared with Fe–N-G. It discloses the higher stability of N-containing sites (defect structure or vacancy) in graphene lattice that maintain in a further thermal process. On the other hand, the peak area of hydroxyls (286.3 eV) increases in Fe–N-GQDs in comparison with Fe–N-G, denoting the retention and increase of oxygen-containing group content at the edge of Fe–N-GQDs. This result is consistent with that deduced from the FTIR spectrum. The absence of a peak at 283.3 eV assigned to C–Fe refused the probability of Fe3O4 particle formation and covalent bonding on the graphene surface (Liu et al., 2016). Furthermore, the O/C atomic ratio for Fe–N-G is less than 1%, which is lower than that in Fe–N-GQDs (26%), indicating the reduction of Fe–N-G and strong edge effect in GQDs. It is noted that the reduction mainly happens on the surface of Fe–N-GQDs by removing the active epoxy, while most hydroxyl and carboxyl groups at the edge still exist (Liu et al., 2013).

Four various types of N 1s XPS peaks are commonly considered relying on the local chemical environment of N including pyridinic N (Npyr), pyrrolic N (Npyrr), graphitic N (Ng), and nitrogen oxide N (N–O). All peaks for Npyr (398.5 eV), Npyrr (401 eV), Ng (403.3 eV), and N–O (405.4 eV) are observed for Fe–N-G (Fig. 2d). The strong peaks for Npyr and Npyrr show the successful formation of porphyrin sites in the graphene matrix. The N–O peak illustrates N oxides in the edge of graphene matrix. After the solvothermal process for the synthesis of Fe–N-GQD, the N 1s peaks for Npyr (399.7 eV), Npyrr (401.3 eV), and N–O (406.3 eV) are obvious (Fig. 2e). The peak of Ng is absent in Fe–N-GQDs demonstrating higher stability of other N types in comparison with graphitic N.

The high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectra of Fe–N-G and Fe–N-GQDs are shown in Fig. 2f. The binding energy at 710.6 eV can be assigned to Fe 2p3/2 of Fe(II) and the peaks at 723.5 eV are consistent with the Fe 2p1/2 binding energy of Fe(II) spectrum (Faraji et al., 2018). In the Fe–N-G N 1s spectrum, the outstanding Npyrr peak (398.5 eV) demonstrates that pyrrolic N is the predominant defect species, while no tangible Fe 2p3/2 peak is detected. The eminent pyridinic N peak along with the Fe 2p3/2 peaks in the synthesized Fe–N-GQD is related to the thermal stability of porphyrin-like divacancy defects in GQDs. To specify the formation of porphyrin-like Fe–N4 divacancy sites, the ratio of XPS peak area of pyridinic N and the Fe 2p3/2 peaks in the Fe–N-G and Fe–N-GQDs was computed to be about 4:1 (Lee et al., 2011).

The high oxygen content of ca. 26% led to the high solubility of the Fe–N-GQDs in aqueous solution (Fig. 2g). The oxygen groups such as the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the Fe–N-GQD surface provide a proper surface for further modifications like biomolecule conjugation (Xu et al., 2015). Moreover, a comparison of high-resolution N 1s and Fe 2p spectra in Fe–N-GQDs with parent Fe–N-G materials shows the binding energy of N and Fe species shift to a relative high-energy direction (Fig. 2d-f). These outcomes disclose the more stabilization of Fe–N-GQDs than Fe–N-G, which is related to the quantum confinement effects and also thermodynamic stability of porphyrin-like divacancy defects in GQDs. The high-resolution C 1s, N 1s, and Fe 2p spectra confirm that the Fe and N atoms were successfully doped in graphene and GQDs. Besides, the doped atoms represent additional electrons to GQDs, which facilitate the radiative relaxation pathway and impress the fluorescence properties of Fe–N-GQDs (Liu et al., 2016).

Magnetism of Fe–N-GQDs was surveyed as shown in Fig. S2. To this end, 10 μl of Fe–N-GQDs solution was dried on a glass substrate. The magnetic hysteresis (M–H) curve confirms the strong paramagnetic behaviour of Fe–N-GQDs. The weak diamagnetism of GQDs compared to Fe–N-GQDs indicates that iron doping can induce the transition of GQDs from weak diamagnetism to paramagnetism. The paramagnetism of Fe–N-GQDs may be attributed to the local magnetic moments of dangling bonds (Panich et al., 2001). This bond can generate a localized spin and then convert a graphite sp2 bond into a sp3 bond at the expense of a π bond. Localized spins in iron regions result in the production of paramagnetic centers due to the isolated spin behaviour (Höhne et al., 2008). Moreover, the paramagnetic behaviour and ultra-small size of Fe–N-GQDs make them an attractive candidate to apply in biological and medical fields such as drug delivery and bio-imaging.

GQDs typically show a potent optical absorption in the UV region with an outstretched tail to the visible range (Zang et al., 2016). For the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of a GQD sample, two kinds of peaks can be observed: a π–π* transition peak centered at a wavelength between 200 and 270 nm and another peak between 270 and 390 nm due to the n–π* transition of CO functional groups introduced during the synthesis processes (Wang et al., 2017). The optical property of GQDs has a critical role in their applications. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe–N-GQDs in water shows a peak at ca. 255 nm arising from the π → π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains (Fig. 3a ). A shoulder peak at 275 nm can be attributed to the n–π* transition of CO bonds and the characteristic absorption peak of GQDs. The blue fluorescence was observed when the Fe–N-GQDs were irradiated with LED light (Fig. 3a inset). The PL intensity of Fe–N-GQDs was measured under different excitation wavelengths. It shows a symmetrical and broad PL peak (full width at half maximum of ca. 100 nm) and the excitation-dependent PL behaviour (Fig. 3b). The maximum PL was observed at 430 nm upon 360 nm excitation, which may due to the optical choice of differently sized Fe–N-GQDs and surface defects of Fe–N-GQDs. The PL of Fe–N-GQDs is solvent-sensitive, as shown in Fig. 3c. (Excitation at 360 nm). The PL intensity of Fe–N-GQDs decreases dramatically in DMF in comparison with water as solvents. This solvent effect could be due to the solvent attachment or different emissive traps on the Fe–N-GQDs surface. The PL emission of Fe–N-GQDs containing a different mass ratio of Fe/total reactants were compared (Fig. S3).

The operational stability of the Fe–N-GQDs was also measured by following the alteration of the PL intensity after increasing irradiation time. After irradiation for 1 h, no considerable PL bleaching was detected (Fig. S4a), revealing that Fe–N-GQDs are photo-stable. These dispersions are stable at 4 °C for at least six months (Fig. S4b). The PL of GQDs has eminent stability compared to fluorescent dyes (Sun et al., 2015). The fluorescent properties of the Fe–N-GQDs at different ionic strengths were traced when the concentration of KCl was increased from 0 to 2.0 M. As shown in Fig. S4c, there are no clear alterations in the PL intensities and the peak characteristics. The pH-dependent PL property is also shown in Fig. S4d. Under acidic conditions, the Fe–N-GQDs emit strong PL whereas, under alkaline conditions, the PL is more quenched due to the protonation and deprotonation of the surface defects (Vandarkuzhali et al., 2017). The exact mechanisms responsible for the PL of GQDs have not been elucidated, yet (Zhu et al., 2014).

Quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution (with QY of 54%) was chosen as standard to calculate QY of Fe–N-GQD samples based on Equation (1) (Hu et al., 2013).
(1) Φs = Φr (Is/Ir) (ns/nr) (Ar /As)
Where, Φ, I, A, and n denote QY value, integrated fluorescence emission intensity, absorbance intensity, and refractive index, respectively. Here, the subscripts “r” and “s” refer to QY of reference and Fe–N-GQD sample, respectively. As shown in Table S1, the QY of Fe–N-GQDs was calculated to be ca. 86%. The QYs obtained for Fe–N-GQDs and other nitrogen-doped GQDs are compared in Table S2.

The deconvoluted N 1s peaks of Fe–N-GQDs show a significant increase in Npyr/N (65%). Based on previous literatures, the relationship of quantum yields and Npyr verifies that the Npyr dominates the PL mechanism and brings a higher quantum yield. These results confirm that the high Npyr/N can dramatically enhance the PL property of N-GQDs. This is probably related to pyridinic N sharing one delocalized electron to the π system across the C atoms. The probability of electron excitation for the same excitation energy enhances as a result of the low energy gap between π and π* due to the varied wave function of the delocalized electron in pyridinic N (Permatasari et al., 2016). On the other, the coordination of iron to the pyridinic N atoms in the center of porphyrin structure can significantly improve their stability, which reduces the energy losses of molecular vibrations in PL progress and increases the quantum yield in comparison to other reported N-GQDs (Table S2). The high QY brings opportunities for Fe–N-GQDs to use in practical applications. Herein, Fe–N-GQDs were used as a fluorescence donor in a FRET-based biosensor for detection of S. Typhi Vi antigen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET mechanism for Vi antigen detection
ce:section.ce:para: The possible photoluminescence quenching mechanism between Fe–N-GQDs and GO could be considered as Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Zarei-Ghobadi et al., 2018). The proposed biosensor provides the desired conditions for the FRET mechanism which includes: (i) overlapping the emission spectra of Fe–N-GQDs as an energy donor with the absorption spectra of GO as a quencher. As seen in Fig. 4a , the emission spectrum of Fe–N-GQDs overlaps with the GO absorption spectrum. This affirms the potential of Fe–N-GQDs and GO as a donor-acceptor couple for applying in FRET assay (Tian et al., 2017). (ii) As previously reported (Huang and Liu, 2012; Kim et al., 2010; Tian et al., 2017), the quenching distance of GO is around 23 nm (energy transfer efficiency of GO has (distance)−4 dependence) in which many bioreactions such as DNA hybridization and antibody-based immunological recognition occur. iii) Fe–N-GQDs and GO have a similar sp2 and sp3 conjugate structure (Loh et al., 2010; Zhang et al., 2011), therefore the strong π–π stacking between them may cause to bring them into FRET proximity (Qian et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2013a), and then leads to the photoluminescence quenching of Fe–N-GQDs by GO. Scheme 2 represents the principle of such an immunosensor. In the FRET mechanism, GO as a fluorescence acceptor can be utilized for quenching the emission of antibody-conjugated Fe–N-GQDs (abbreviated as Fe–N-GQDs/Ab) as donors. Fe–N-GQDs/Ab and GO constitute a stable Fe–N-GQDs/Ab/GO complex through strong π stacking interaction between Fe–N-GQDs/Ab and honeycomb lattices of GO. This results in a remarkable fluorescence quenching (Sun et al., 2013; Zhao et al., 2013b). In the presence of target (Vi Ag), the distance between Fe–N-GQDs and GO effectively increases due to the specific antibody–antigen interaction, thus the FRET process is hindered, and PL is recovered. This concept was further confirmed by the fluorescence spectroscopies in Fig. 4. When Fe–N-GQDs/Ab is adsorbed on the surface of GO, the fluorescence of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab would be quenched owing to FRET assay (Fig. 4b, blue line). Next, by addition of Vi antigen, it binds to the antibody (anti-Vi antigen) due to the specific antibody–antigen interaction to constitute Fe–N-GQDs/Ab-Ag. This leads to an increase in the distance between Fe–N-GQDs/Ab and GO, hindering the FRET process and recovering the fluorescence emission (Fig. 4b, purple line). The kinetics of the fluorescence quenching was explored by Stern-Volmer (SV) equation, F0/F = 1 + Ksv C (Lakowicz, 2013): Where, C is the concentration of GO, Ksv is the SV quenching constants, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the Fe–N GQDs/Ab without GO and with various concentrations of GO, respectively. Ksv was calculated based on the PL intensity changes of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab versus concentration of GO at three temperatures (23, 30, and 37 °C). According to (Zu et al., 2017), the rise of temperature causes the decrease of Ksv in static quenching and increases in dynamic quenching. Fig. S5 shows that Ksv is reversely proportional to the temperature, disclosing that the Fe–N-GQDs/Ab/GO system is a static quenching process. After excitation, the non-radiative energy transition is occurred from the donor to GO via dipole-dipole interaction (Saini et al., 2006).

As Fig. 4c shows, the PL intensity of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab is decreased with increasing concentration of GO confirming the quenching effect of GO due to energy or electron transfer from Fe–N-GQDs to GO. The inset in Fig. 4c demonstrates that the PL quenching of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab reaches a plateau in the proximity of GO with concentration exceeded 10 μg/ml. Therefore, it was used as the optimized GO concentration for further experiments.

For further assessment of the fluorescence recovery, the relative recovery rate ((Fr-Fq)/Fq) was determined, in which Fr is the recovered fluorescence intensity after the addition of Vi antigen and Fq is the fluorescence intensity of the Fe–N-GQD-Ab/GO assay after quenching. The overall fluorescence response was relying on the amount of Vi antigen in the samples; therefore, the assay can be effectively employed for the quantitative determination of Vi antigen. To examine this, various concentrations of Vi antigen were added to the Fe–N-GQD-Ab/GO solution which resulted in recovering PL intensity due to the formation of the Fe–N-GQDs/Ab-Ag/GO complex (Fig. 4d). The subsequent addition of Vi antigen led to the formation of a large number of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab-Ag/GO complexes. This in turn caused escaping GO from the surface and enhancing PL intensity. The inset of Fig. 4d indicates the linear relationship between the relative fluorescence signal recovery rate against the logarithm of Vi antigen concentrations (y = 0.467 + 0.807 log [Vi Ag]) with regression coefficient (R2) 0.992. The limit of detection (LOD) of Vi antigen was found as 1 pg/mL. The linear range was found to be 1 pg/mL – 10 ng/mL.

The main advantage of this immunosensor is the low LOD (1 pg/mL) which is superior to that of previously reported researches as mentioned in Table S3. The obtained LOD for Vi antigen by applying anti-Vi antibody functionalized gold nanoparticles, anti-Vi antibody functionalized CdTe quantum dots, and aptamer functionalized MoS2-rGO nanocomposite were calculated as 5, 1, and 0.1 ng, respectively (Pandey et al. 2012, 2015; Pathania et al., 2018). The reason for such a high sensitivity may be due to quantum yield and tunability of emission spectrum of donor molecules that are the two important parameters for FRET assays. The high quantum yield of Fe–N-GQDs leads to high energy transfer efficiency. Tunability of emission spectrum of Fe–N-GQDs increases versatility to match with various quencher molecules. Among graphene-based materials, GO is mostly applied as a quencher for FRET-based biological applications (Ghobadi et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2015). This supremacy is ascribed to the unique heterogeneous structure (sp2-sp3) (Gao, 2015), large surface-volume ratio, and good solubility in water. Moreover, the long-range quenching effect (~23 nm) of GO makes it a significant candidate among other traditional fluorescent quenchers (~10 nm) for large biomolecules detection (Huang and Liu, 2012; Loh et al., 2010). The disadvantage of the FRET-based detection strategy is the response time of sensing due to indirect detection by adding GO as a quencher.

To evaluate the specificity of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab towards Vi antigen, the immunosensor response was compared with those obtained for other antigens (S. Typhimurium, E. coli, P. aeruginosa and S. aureus). When non-specific samples were added into the Fe–N-GQDs-Ab/Ab/GO mixture, the recovery rate of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab was not significant relative to that of Vi antigen under the similar conditions (Fig. 4e). This result showed that the immunosensor is specific toward S. Typhi Vi antigen detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Vi antigen in real human serum
ce:section.ce:para: In order to survey the feasibility of the immunosensor for real sample analysis, the particular amounts of Vi antigen were spiked into the healthy human serum. The samples were ultrafiltrated at 3000 rpm for 30 min and then diluted in deionized water (1:10). Next, it was spiked with different concentrations of Vi antigen solutions (1 pg/mL to 1 μg/mL). A blank was also prepared utilizing the diluted human serum. To consider the utility of this immunosensor for real biological sample analysis, the matrix effect was examined. No significant differences were found between the PL intensity of Fe–N-GQDs/Ab in human serum and PBS after changing concentrations of Vi antigen. All obtained results were normalized to the blank sample. As seen in Fig. 4f, the fluorescence recoveries for different concentrations of spiked antigen samples were in the range of 93–107% indicating that the proposed biosensor can be successfully applied for the quantitative detection of Vi antigen in real samples without remarkable interferences of complex matrixes. Furthermore, a relative standard deviation of 6.5% (n = 3, 1 ng/mL Vi antigen) was obtained for this method, indicating the reproducibility of the responses. Also, the recovery rate in the unspiked serum was very low (0.0196). Perhaps, the pre-treatment steps of filtration and dilution were effective in reducing the matrix effect (Fig. 4f).

In the present study, a novel iron porphyrin-like Fe–N-GQDs with remarkable paramagnetic property and PL intensity was introduced. The synthesized paramagnetic iron porphyrin-like Fe–N-GQDs present new structural features leading to prominent quantum yield due to outstanding pyridinic nitrogen. Also, Fe and N atoms doped in GQDs represent additional electrons which facilitate the radiative relaxation pathway and impress the fluorescence properties of Fe–N-GQDs. The high oxygen functional groups at the surface led to the high solubility of the Fe–N-GQDs in an aqueous solution and provided a proper surface for further modifications like antibody conjugation, as well. Bio-conjugated F–N-GQDs as a prominent FRET donor was successfully applied for detection of S. Typhi Vi antigen with good sensitivity and selectivity. The design has the potential to be utilized to detect other antigens via the substitution of antibodies. It is expected that this high photoluminescence of Fe-N-GQDs besides paramagnetic behaviour encourages the more application of carbon-based nanomaterials in biology and the medical fields.

Zahra Kamal: Conceptualization, Data curation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Mohadeseh Zarei Ghobadi: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Seyed Majid Mohseni: Methodology, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Hedayatollah Ghourchian: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the Research Council of the University of Tehran.
